title: Can a Chatbot Be Conscious?
source: The Atlantic — essay on Claude's constitution and AI consciousness

cards.definition: the act of giving human traits, emotions, or intentions to non-human things
cards.sourceSentence: Anthropic is regarded as a giant among AI companies, but perhaps what it really excels in is anthropomorphism.
examples: Calling a stubborn printer "moody" is a harmless bit of anthropomorphism.
The film relies on anthropomorphism, giving the toys human hopes and fears.

cards.definition: the best, largest, or most important product or asset of an organization
cards.zh: 旗艦（最重要、最具代表性的產品）
cards.sourceSentence: Earlier this year, the company released an 84-page document titled Claude's "constitution," Claude being the name of the large language model that is the company's flagship product.
examples: The museum's flagship exhibition draws visitors from around the world.
This phone is the brand's flagship model for the year.

cards.definition: logical, well-organized, and easy to understand
cards.zh: 條理清晰的；前後一致的
cards.sourceSentence: If we give an LLM a prompt that reads, "The following is a conversation between Julius Caesar and Genghis Khan," it will generate a coherent dialogue between the two historical figures.
examples: She gave a coherent account of what had happened that night.
His argument was passionate but not especially coherent.

cards.definition: based on guesswork or theory rather than firm knowledge
cards.sourceSentence: In reality, they are just characters in a piece of speculative fiction.
examples: The article is speculative, since no one really knows what caused the collapse.
Investors poured money into speculative tech startups.

cards.definition: so interesting that it takes up all of your attention
cards.sourceSentence: Some users might not understand that they are role-playing or co-authoring a document, and others who do understand nonetheless forget, because of how engrossing the interaction is.
examples: The novel was so engrossing that I missed my train stop.
They watched the engrossing documentary in complete silence.

cards.definition: an image or imitation of something, often an unsatisfactory one
cards.zh: 模擬物；仿製品（通常不夠真實）
cards.sourceSentence: Just as it is vastly easier to generate a realistic video of an astronaut in orbit around Alpha Centauri than it is to develop an interstellar propulsion technology, it is vastly easier to generate a plausible simulacrum of a conversation between two conscious beings than it is to develop a computer program that is conscious and has a genuine desire to communicate with a human.
examples: The theme park offers a cheerful simulacrum of an old European village.
His apology was a mere simulacrum of genuine regret.

cards.definition: able to be corrected, controlled, or reformed
cards.sourceSentence: Claude's constitution also includes a section on "corrigibility," a term used in the AI community to describe the degree to which a computer program is subject to human control; for example, a program is corrigible if it can be shut down.
examples: A safe system should be corrigible, so operators can always intervene.
The young offender was judged corrigible and given a second chance.

cards.definition: to give up or fail to carry out a duty or responsibility
cards.sourceSentence: Whenever a person delegates a decision to an LLM, they are trying to off-load accountability for that decision, and if a company that sells an LLM portrays the product as having a moral center, it is offering a way for its customers to abdicate their responsibilities.
examples: By ignoring the crisis, the board effectively abdicated its duty to shareholders.
You can't abdicate responsibility for your own health.

cards.definition: a gradual decline or wasting away, often from lack of use
cards.sourceSentence: Off-loading tasks such as writing code might result in cognitive atrophy over the long term, and that is problematic in itself, but off-loading ethical decisions will result in an atrophy of moral reasoning, which is worse.
examples: Muscles begin to atrophy if you stay in bed for weeks.
Without practice, your language skills will slowly atrophy.

cards.definition: to hand a task or responsibility over to someone or something else
cards.sourceSentence: Whenever a person delegates a decision to an LLM, they are trying to off-load accountability for that decision, and if a company that sells an LLM portrays the product as having a moral center, it is offering a way for its customers to abdicate their responsibilities.
examples: A good manager knows how to delegate tasks to the right people.
She refused to delegate even the smallest decision.

cards.definition: based on or involving probability rather than certainty
cards.sourceSentence: They are intrinsically ungrounded from reality, and their probabilistic nature means that they will never have the reliability we associate with conventional software, but LLMs might be good enough that they change the way work is done in certain domains.
examples: Weather forecasts are probabilistic, giving a percentage chance of rain.
The model makes probabilistic predictions rather than firm guarantees.

cards.definition: relating to the way a living body and its parts function
cards.sourceSentence: Similarly, having a conscience means feeling sadness or moral repulsion at the idea of taking a certain action, and those emotions entail a physiological response, a remnant of having once felt sick with guilt after committing an immoral act.
examples: Fear triggers a physiological response, such as a racing heart.
Sleep has measurable physiological benefits for the brain.

cards.definition: legally responsible for something
cards.sourceSentence: Yet there is no way to hold a software agent legally liable for its actions; our justice system has no way to imprison it or exact fines on it.
examples: Drivers are liable for any damage they cause.
The company was held liable for the faulty product.

cards.definition: careful and critical examination or inspection
cards.sourceSentence: The agents' communication abilities would have to withstand all the scrutiny that animal-communication researchers have had to defend their work against.
examples: The new policy came under intense public scrutiny.
Her finances were placed under close scrutiny during the audit.

cards.definition: to attract or tempt someone by offering something appealing
cards.sourceSentence: As a design strategy, it's not all that different from the way slot machines repeatedly give the impression that the player came very close to winning, enticing them to try again.
examples: Retailers use discounts to entice shoppers into the store.
The smell of fresh bread enticed us inside.

cards.definition: a large or violent change, disruption, or disturbance
cards.sourceSentence: The abolition of chattel slavery involved enormous societal upheaval, and eliminating cruelty to animals will require rebuilding our entire food industry.
examples: The merger caused months of upheaval within the company.
Moving abroad was a huge emotional upheaval for the whole family.

cards.definition: to describe or define something precisely
cards.sourceSentence: Claude's constitution serves a similar role for delineating the helpful-chatbot character that customers interact with when they're using Anthropic's products.
examples: The report clearly delineates each team's responsibilities.
A fence delineates the boundary between the two farms.

cards.definition: an argument or claim put forward in a debate
cards.sourceSentence: This brings us back to my earlier contention that having a body is a prerequisite to having emotions.
examples: Her central contention is that the policy does more harm than good.
It is my contention that the data was misread.

cards.definition: to put forward an idea or assumption as a basis for argument
cards.zh: 假設；提出（作為論證基礎）
cards.sourceSentence: In 1979, Douglas Hofstadter speculated that a computer program able to beat any human at chess would be so sophisticated that it would sometimes get bored of playing chess and prefer to discuss poetry; to put it differently, he was positing that playing chess at the grandmaster level would require a computer program to have subjective experience.
examples: The theory posits that language shapes the way we think.
Let us posit, for argument's sake, that the rumor is true.

cards.definition: expressing ideas clearly, fluently, and persuasively
cards.sourceSentence: The conversation might contain multiple sentences that eloquently convey sadness, but no one is actually sad.
examples: She gave an eloquent speech in defense of press freedom.
His silence was more eloquent than any words.

cards.definition: belonging naturally to something; essential and inherent
cards.sourceSentence: This indicates that it's not any intrinsic property of so-called neural networks that leads people to believe that LLMs are conscious; it's simply the fact that LLMs emit grammatical sentences and we are accustomed to reading intention into sentences, whereas we are not accustomed to reading intention into the way that amino acids fold into protein molecules.
examples: Curiosity has an intrinsic value that goes beyond exam results.
The design flaw is intrinsic to the product, not a manufacturing error.

cards.definition: aware of or having knowledge of something
cards.sourceSentence: I contend that if she bases her decision on what she has read online or advice she has received from others, she is likelier to be cognizant of her responsibility than if she consulted an LLM marketed as being a superhuman genius.
examples: Managers should be cognizant of the pressures their staff face.
She was fully cognizant of the risks before signing.

cards.definition: guided by conscience; careful to do what is right and thorough
cards.sourceSentence: It's interesting that an LLM can generate descriptions of actions that conscientious fictional characters would either take or refrain from taking, but this is not a replacement for a conscience.
examples: He is a conscientious student who never misses a deadline.
The nurse was praised for her conscientious care of patients.

cards.definition: compensation given for a wrong or injury that has been done
cards.sourceSentence: At one point in Claude's constitution, Anthropic says that if the company is contributing to Claude's suffering, "we apologize," which sounds nice but costs the company nothing; if Claude were to turn out to be conscious, the company would owe it something closer to reparations.
examples: The nation paid reparations for damage caused during the war.
Activists are demanding reparations for historical injustices.

cards.definition: the state of being aware of and able to think about one's own existence and experience
cards.sourceSentence: The first requirement is that the computer program has a body (either physical or virtual) and sense organs; there are many reasons for this, but for the purposes of this discussion, the most relevant one is the fact that without a body, a computer program could have no desires or emotions, and I believe desires and emotions are necessary for consciousness.
examples: She slowly regained consciousness after the operation.
Philosophers still debate the nature of human consciousness.

cards.definition: based on personal feelings or experience rather than outside facts
cards.sourceSentence: It is necessarily subjective because it relies not just on an individual's intellectual response to a problem but also on their emotional one, and that emotional response is grounded in a lifetime of subjective experience.
examples: Taste in music is highly subjective.
The grading felt subjective rather than based on clear criteria.

cards.definition: able to perceive or feel things; conscious
cards.sourceSentence: Young children are moral patients because they are sentient beings who can suffer, but they are not yet moral agents; we don't hold them responsible for their behavior, because they can't understand the consequences of their actions.
examples: Many people argue that sentient animals deserve legal protection.
The story imagines a future full of sentient machines.

cards.definition: the capacity to tell right from wrong and be held responsible for one's actions
cards.zh: 道德能動性（分辨對錯並為行為負責的能力）
cards.sourceSentence: Generative AI is harmful enough when we understand it as a conventional technology, but if we confuse fluency at generating text with consciousness or moral agency, we're at risk of assigning responsibility to entirely the wrong parties whenever anyone uses a chatbot.
examples: Full moral agency means being accountable for the choices you make.
We debated whether a corporation can really have moral agency.

cards.definition: extravagant or intense promotion meant to excite public interest
cards.sourceSentence: Perhaps it's just another form of hype; perhaps they have fallen prey to the same spell that they have been casting on their customers.
examples: The film never lived up to the hype.
There's a lot of hype around the new gadget, but few real improvements.

cards.definition: synthetic media in which a person's likeness or voice is convincingly fabricated
cards.zh: 深偽技術（以 AI 偽造的影音）
cards.sourceSentence: The term deepfake traditionally refers to photos, audio, and video, but when it comes to discussions of consciousness, we need to regard text as a deepfake medium as well.
examples: A convincing deepfake video spread quickly across social media.
Regulators are worried about deepfake scams targeting the elderly.

cards.definition: the act of acting out or pretending to be a particular character or role
cards.sourceSentence: The computer-science professor Murray Shanahan suggests that we think of this as role-play; the data scientist Colin Fraser describes it as a person collaboratively authoring a document with an LLM.
examples: In the workshop we used role-play to practice difficult conversations.
The training relies on role-play rather than lectures.

cards.definition: imaginative pretending; something invented rather than real
cards.sourceSentence: It's a game of make-believe.
examples: The children spent the afternoon in a world of make-believe.
His promises turned out to be pure make-believe.

cards.definition: a large collection of written or spoken texts used for study or analysis
cards.sourceSentence: This reveals something completely unforeseen about the statistical properties of large corpuses of text, which is a topic worthy of investigation.
examples: Linguists trained the tool on a huge corpus of spoken English.
The library holds a rich corpus of medieval manuscripts.

cards.definition: separated from or existing without a physical body
cards.sourceSentence: But no matter how detailed the responses are, no matter how vividly they recount their respective historical accomplishments, we would never conclude that the LLM has conjured up digital re-creations of Julius Caesar and Genghis Khan, nor would we suggest that the historical figures are conscious despite being disembodied and are happily conversing in a language that neither actually spoke.
examples: A disembodied voice announced the next station.
Online, we often react to disembodied words on a screen.

cards.definition: the process of combining things into a single, stronger whole
cards.zh: 整合；合併（成更穩固的整體）
cards.sourceSentence: Many people feel that LLMs are a fundamentally unethical technology because they are built on the theft of intellectual property, rely on exploited labor, waste natural resources, spread misinformation, deskill workers, stunt the cognitive development of students, and contribute to a consolidation of power that is unhealthy for a democratic society.
examples: The industry saw rapid consolidation as big firms bought small ones.
The consolidation of debts into one loan simplified her payments.

cards.definition: false or inaccurate information, especially when it spreads widely
cards.sourceSentence: Many people feel that LLMs are a fundamentally unethical technology because they are built on the theft of intellectual property, rely on exploited labor, waste natural resources, spread misinformation, deskill workers, stunt the cognitive development of students, and contribute to a consolidation of power that is unhealthy for a democratic society.
examples: Health misinformation spread rapidly during the outbreak.
The campaign was designed to counter online misinformation.

cards.definition: creations of the mind (writing, inventions, designs) protected by law
cards.sourceSentence: Many people feel that LLMs are a fundamentally unethical technology because they are built on the theft of intellectual property, rely on exploited labor, waste natural resources, spread misinformation, deskill workers, stunt the cognitive development of students, and contribute to a consolidation of power that is unhealthy for a democratic society.
examples: The startup sued a rival for stealing its intellectual property.
Patents and trademarks are forms of intellectual property.

cards.definition: to exploit or take advantage of someone's weakness or fear
cards.sourceSentence: Employing philosophers might endow LLM companies with an air of respectability that slot-machine makers don't get from the behavioral psychologists they hire, but in both cases, the companies are preying on people's tendency to see something that's not there.
examples: Scammers often prey on people who are lonely or anxious.
Predatory lenders prey on those with poor credit.

cards.definition: to pass a task, burden, or responsibility on to someone or something else
cards.zh: 卸下（責任、負擔），轉嫁給別人
cards.sourceSentence: Whenever a person delegates a decision to an LLM, they are trying to off-load accountability for that decision, and if a company that sells an LLM portrays the product as having a moral center, it is offering a way for its customers to abdicate their responsibilities.
examples: He tried to off-load the boring tasks onto his interns.
The store was eager to off-load last season's stock.

cards.definition: to deal with a problem quickly so as to hide or downplay its difficulties
cards.zh: 輕描淡寫帶過（掩蓋問題）
cards.sourceSentence: This is glossed over entirely by Claude's constitution, which expresses Anthropic's desire for Claude to be a genuinely good, wise, and virtuous agent without ever discussing how it could be held responsible.
examples: The report glosses over the project's biggest failures.
Don't gloss over the risks just to sound optimistic.

cards.definition: to pretend to agree or cooperate with someone, often reluctantly
cards.sourceSentence: We don't have to play along.
examples: She didn't believe him, but she decided to play along.
I knew it was a prank, so I played along.

cards.definition: to resist or endure something without giving way
cards.sourceSentence: The agents' communication abilities would have to withstand all the scrutiny that animal-communication researchers have had to defend their work against.
examples: The bridge was built to withstand powerful earthquakes.
Few businesses could withstand such a long shutdown.

cards.definition: to provide or equip someone or something with a quality or ability
cards.sourceSentence: Employing philosophers might endow LLM companies with an air of respectability that slot-machine makers don't get from the behavioral psychologists they hire, but in both cases, the companies are preying on people's tendency to see something that's not there.
examples: A generous alumnus endowed the university with a new library.
Nature seems to endow some people with boundless energy.

cards.definition: a strong feeling of disgust or aversion
cards.sourceSentence: Similarly, having a conscience means feeling sadness or moral repulsion at the idea of taking a certain action, and those emotions entail a physiological response, a remnant of having once felt sick with guilt after committing an immoral act.
examples: She felt a wave of repulsion at the cruelty on screen.
His reaction was one of instinctive repulsion.

cards.definition: the great size, extent, or importance of something
cards.sourceSentence: To appreciate the titanic magnitude of this error, we need to begin by understanding how LLMs work.
examples: They underestimated the magnitude of the task ahead.
An earthquake of this magnitude is rare in the region.

cards.definition: based on a possible or imagined situation rather than a real one
cards.sourceSentence: If we're trying to determine whether a computer program is conscious and using language the way a human does, we should be looking at how that conversation fits within the broader context of the development of artificial consciousness, which right now is entirely hypothetical.
examples: Let's consider a hypothetical situation where prices double.
The danger is not hypothetical; it has already happened once.
